exemplar.title: To what extent did the digital economy influence consumer behavior in India before, during, and after the COVID-19 lockdown?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent did the digital economy influence consumer behavior in India before, during, and after the COVID-19 lockdown?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated and effectively frames the investigationâs scope.
Investigation focus is consistently referenced throughout the essay.
Appropriate use of secondary national data sources to address the question.
weaknesses: Objectives 2.2 and 2.4 stray into labour-market issues beyond consumer behaviour.
Methodology lacks defined inclusion/exclusion criteria for source selection.
Some cited statistics are not traced back to listed reports.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates a clear, focused research question and maintains relevance to consumer behaviour in the digital economy. The choice of secondary data sources is broadly appropriate, although unevenâno primary data were gathered and some references remain vague. While the investigation structure and question alignment merit a high mark within this criterion, gaps in methodological rigor and occasional drift prevent full marks. A score of 5/6 reflects strong performance with room for tightening focus and clarifying source-selection criteria.

strengths: Tables, graphs and quotations (e.g., UPI growth, GDP series) are generally well chosen and relevant.
Solid factual knowledge of macro-trends and digital-payments ecosystem.
Frequent use of economic terminology that is mostly accurate and appropriate.
weaknesses: Some figures are copied without critical comment and a few claims lack citations.
Unit labels in tables (e.g., millions vs. billions) are inconsistent.
Vague citation of âIB economics textbooksâ without full bibliographic details.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates clear knowledge of the topic and mostly relevant application of source material. Graphical data are deployed at appropriate points, and terminology is used with a high degree of accuracy. However, occasional numerical inaccuracies, incomplete citations, and vague source attributions limit the clarity and coherence of understanding. A mid-band score of 4/6 acknowledges competent knowledge use while signalling the need for more precise citation and data verification.

strengths: Majority of sources are reputable and tied directly to each sectionâs purpose.
Analysis includes percentage changes, IQR and Z-scores with clear links to consumer-behaviour shifts.
Argument recognises limitations and explores alternative policies.
weaknesses: Mathematical errors (cube-root in YoY formula, data-entry mismatches) undermine some conclusions.
Evaluation of data reliability and counter-arguments is brief and non-critical.
Limited modelling beyond standard demand-shift diagrams.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical thinking is consistently solid: research relevance is high, analysis is clear, and conclusions are mostly supported. The student goes beyond description by calculating dispersion measures and connecting quantitative trends to behaviour. Yet recurring calculation errors, underdeveloped critique of sourcesâ reliability, and minimal counter-argumentation prevent elevating this to the 7-9 band. A score of 5/12 reflects evident analytical strength tempered by the need for deeper critical evaluation and error-free computation.

strengths: Structure follows standard EE conventions with logical headings and a clear contents outline.
Includes essential sections (abstract, methodology, conclusion, bibliography).
Use of tables and charts supports readability.
weaknesses: Figures are low-resolution and lack clear axis labels.
Citation style and mathematical formatting are inconsistent.
Word count slightly exceeds the 4,000-word limit and page numbers are missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is generally coherent: the reportâs structure and organization allow the reader to follow the argument. However, inconsistent layout details (image quality, caption placement), exceeded word count, missing page numbers, and variable citation formatting indicate that not all conventions are fully met. A score of 3/4 reflects a clearly appropriate structure with several areas for refinement.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is reproduced verbatim at the start and is narrow enoughâlimited to India, consumer behaviour, and the digital economy across three specific time-period groupingsâto guide systematic investigation.  Data selection, tables and sub-sections are all explicitly linked back to this question, showing a strong connection between question and discussion.  Occasional drift into wider labour-market issues does not significantly blur the focus.

The investigationâs focus on the influence of the digital economy on Indian consumer behaviour is introduced early, repeated in the objectives, and consistently referenced throughout the essay.  The student defines five clear phases (âFar Before â¦ Far After COVIDâ), signalling an intention to structure the analysis chronologically.  While minor wording inconsistencies appear later, the central idea remains unmistakable and appropriate for economic analysis, so the communication is effective if not flawless.

Tables, graphs and quotations are generally well chosen (GDP series, UPI growth, unemployment, etc.) and are deployed at appropriate points in the argument, demonstrating relevance.  Nevertheless some figures are copied without critical comment (e.g., cube-root error in YoY equation) and a few claims lack citations, so application is âmostlyâ but not âclearlyâ relevant.

Standard EE structural conventions (abstract/word count, contents outline, numbered sections, conclusion, bibliography) are present.  Headings and sub-headings logically guide the reader, though minor duplication and occasional abrupt page breaks show that refinement could be improved.  Overall the structure is clearly appropriate.

Basic layout elements are in place (tables, charts, in-text citations), but presentation is inconsistent: figures are low-resolution, some mathematical expressions are corrupted, and citation style varies.  Word count slightly exceeds the 4,000-word limit, and page numbers are absent in the extract.  These shortcomings indicate that âsomeâ but not all layout conventions are met.

The essay builds a coherent argument linking digitalisation to consumer behaviour and GDP.  It recognises limitations and alternative policies, providing some evaluation.  However critique of data reliability and counter-arguments is brief, and the synthesis of quantitative and qualitative insights is uneven, so evaluation is âpartially criticalâ rather than fully developed.

Analytical sections calculate percentage changes, IQR, Z-scores and link shifts in GDP components to behavioural changes, showing clear reasoning.  Nonetheless the maths is occasionally flawed (cube-root in YoY) and there is limited modelling beyond demand-shift diagrams.  Conclusions are mostly but not always fully substantiated, placing the work comfortably in 4-6 but short of 7-9.

The student shows solid factual knowledge of Indian macro-trends, the digital-payments ecosystem and standard macro concepts (GDP components, MPC).  However some statements are descriptive and a handful of numerical inaccuracies appear.  Source use is generally integrated but not always synthesised, keeping the quality at a competent, mid-band level.

The bulk of sources are reputable national or international publications, and the majority are tied directly to each sub-sectionâs purpose.  Some peripheral citations (blogs, newspaper snippets) appear, yet overall research relevance is more than partial and approaches comprehensive, warranting the upper half of the 4-6 band.

A largely secondary-data methodology is described and relevant national sources (RBI, NSO, NPCI, BCG, Statista, etc.) are tabulated.  The selection is appropriate but uneven: no primary data are gathered despite consumer-behaviour claims, and some references (e.g., âIB economics textbooksâ) are vague.  The essay later relies on statistics that are not always traced back to the listed reports, indicating only âsome evidenceâ of informed selection.

Economic terminologyâUPI, GFCF, MPC, IQR, Z-score, digital divideâis frequent and mostly accurate.  Minor lapses (mis-typed formulae, odd use of overlines/Greek letters) prevent full consistency, but the command of vocabulary surpasses the âmostly accurateâ threshold.

annotations.comment: Word count exceeds the 4 000-word limit (4057 words). Reduce content or tighten analysis to comply with IB requirements.
The contents outline is logically structured with clear sections and numbered headings, following EE conventions and aiding navigation.
Research question is clearly stated and effectively frames the investigationâs scope across the three time periods, guiding systematic analysis.
Using IQR on only five annual observations may not provide robust measures of dispersion. Justify this choice or consider alternative analysis.
The figure caption is split across two heading elements. Combine into a single caption block directly beneath the figure for cohesion.
Notation in the percentage formula is inconsistent (âOld Gdpâ vs. âOld GDPâ). Standardise variable names and define symbols clearly.
The percentage change is applied to values in crore rupees but unit consistency is not addressed. Confirm unit conversion when computing growth rates.
Table 4 title spans two lines and the header is truncated. Adjust text box or font size so the full caption is visible.
The claim of a 105% increase in digital payments lacks clarity on whether this is transaction count, value or share. Define the metric explicitly.
Table header indicates âVolume in Millionâ¹â but values appear in billions. Verify and correct unit labels to maintain data accuracy.
In-text citations for quotations lack page numbers and detailed source information. Include full reference details (author, year, page) for each quote.
Caption is placed after the table but is not explicitly referenced in the text. Number each table and refer to it (e.g., âsee Table 1â) to improve reader guidance.
Research objectives 2.2 and 2.4 stray into labor-market and policy evaluation beyond consumer behaviour. Tighten objectives to maintain focus on the research question.
Methodology overview mentions secondary data use but lacks clear criteria for source selection. Define inclusion/exclusion parameters to demonstrate rigor.
Listing âIB economics textbooksâ without title, edition, or author is vague. Specify exact sources to allow verification and strengthen theoretical grounding.
GDP contraction figures are cited but lack critical discussion of data reliability or differences between provisional and final estimates.
The identical +6.1% growth rate for two nonadjacent periods indicates a likely data entry error. Verify original figures to ensure accuracy.
Computed mean (Âµ) and standard deviation (Ï) appear inconsistent with the five GDP values. Recalculate these summary statistics accurately.
Figure 1 is low resolution and axis labels are difficult to read. Use a higher-quality image and ensure all axes, units, and legends are clear.
A cube-root transformation in YoY growth formula is incorrect. Use the standard percentage change formula with raw values to ensure valid growth rates.
Providing a USD figure (US$ 7 billion) for retailer sales would be strengthened by converting to INR or providing market context.
BigBasketâs reported 36% revenue increase lacks baseline context. Clarify whether this refers to year-on-year growth, absolute share, or another metric.
Calculation of rural internet users (186 million) yields a slight mismatch with 504 million total users. Re-evaluate figures or sourcing for consistency.
Demand-shift graph (Figure 3) lacks axis labels and units. Annotate axes with price (â¹) and quantity metrics to improve interpretability.
Section title contains a stray quotation mark (âInterests Post COVID-19ââ). Remove extraneous punctuation for clean formatting.
Listing UPI and mobile-wallet growth clearly demonstrates relevant source application and understanding of digital-economy concepts.
Equation formatting shows artifacts (overline Ï, âCoreâ) that obscure meaning. Retype or correct symbols for clarity in statistical expressions.
Table of private consumption is comprehensive; integrate brief commentary interpreting percentage changes in relation to consumer behaviour.
Typographical error âincreased sayingsâ should read âincreased savings.â Correct such mistakes to maintain clarity and professionalism.
Government-spending calculation includes âcoreâ decimals that are unclear. Standardise unit notation to match table presentation.
